content:
# Acts Chapter 21 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 251-253 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_021_page_001.jpg, ACT_021_page_002.jpg, ACT_021_page_003.jpg

---

**1** After we had torn ourselves away from them, we put out to sea and sailed a straight course to Cos. The next day we reached Rhodes, and from there went on to Patara.

**2** Finding a ship crossing over to Phoenicia, we boarded it and set sail.

**3** We came in sight of Cyprus, passed it on our left, and sailed on to Syria. We landed at Tyre, where the ship was to unload its cargo.

**4** We sought out the disciples there and stayed with them seven days. Through the Spirit they kept urging Paul not to set foot in Jerusalem.

**5** But when our time was up, we left and continued on our way. All of them, with their wives and children, walked us out of the city. There on the beach we knelt down and prayed together.

**6** Then we said our goodbyes to each other, climbed aboard the ship, and they turned back home.

**7** Continuing our voyage from Tyre, we put in at Ptolemais, where we greeted the brothers and sisters and spent one day with them.

**8** The next day we left and came to Caesarea. We went to the home of Philip the evangelist, one of the Seven, and stayed with him.

**9** He had four unmarried daughters who prophesied.

**10** After we had been there a number of days, a prophet named Agabus came down from Judea.

**11** He came to us, took Paul's belt, and tied his own hands and feet with it. Then he declared, "The Holy Spirit says this: 'The man who owns this belt — this is how the Jewish leaders in Jerusalem will bind him and hand him over to the Gentiles.'"

**12** When we heard this, we and the local believers pleaded with Paul not to go up to Jerusalem.

**13** But Paul answered, "What are you doing, weeping and breaking my heart like this? I am ready not only to be bound but to die in Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus."

**14** When he would not be persuaded, we gave up and said, "The Lord's will be done."

**15** After these days we packed up and headed for Jerusalem.

**16** Some of the disciples from Caesarea came along with us and brought us to the home of Mnason of Cyprus, an early disciple, where we were to stay.

**17** When we arrived in Jerusalem, the brothers and sisters welcomed us warmly.

**18** The next day Paul went with us to see James, and all the elders were there.

**19** Paul greeted them and then gave a detailed account of everything God had done among the Gentiles through his ministry.

**20** When they heard it, they praised God. Then they said to him, "You see, brother, how many thousands of Jews have come to believe, and every one of them is zealous for the Law.

**21** But they have been told about you — that you teach all the Jews living among the Gentiles to abandon Moses, telling them not to circumcise their children or follow the customs.

**22** So what do we do? They will certainly hear that you have come.

**23** Here is what we suggest: We have four men who are under a vow.

**24** Take them along, go through the purification rite with them, and pay for them to have their heads shaved. Then everyone will know that the reports about you are groundless and that you yourself live in keeping with the Law.

**25** As for the Gentile believers, we have already written our decision: they should stay away from food sacrificed to idols, from blood, from the meat of strangled animals, and from sexual immorality."

**26** So the next day Paul took the men, purified himself along with them, and went to the temple to give notice of the date when the days of purification would be completed and the offering made for each of them.

**27** When the seven days were nearly over, some Jews from the province of Asia spotted Paul in the temple. They whipped the whole crowd into a frenzy and grabbed him,

**28** shouting, "Men of Israel, help! This is the man who teaches everyone everywhere against our people, our Law, and this sacred place. And on top of that, he has brought Greeks into the temple and defiled this holy ground!"

**29** (They had previously seen Trophimus the Ephesian in the city with Paul and assumed Paul had brought him into the temple.)

**30** The whole city erupted. People came running from every direction. They seized Paul and dragged him out of the temple, and immediately the gates were slammed shut behind him.

**31** They were trying to kill him when word reached the commander of the Roman garrison that all Jerusalem was in chaos.

**32** He immediately took soldiers and centurions and charged down to the crowd. When the mob saw the commander and his troops, they stopped beating Paul.

**33** The commander came up, arrested Paul, and ordered him bound with two chains. Then he demanded to know who he was and what he had done.

**34** But in the crowd, some were shouting one thing, some another. Since the commander could not get at the truth because of the uproar, he ordered Paul taken into the barracks.

**35** When Paul reached the steps, the violence of the mob was so intense that the soldiers had to carry him.

**36** The mass of people kept pressing in behind, screaming, "Away with him!"

**37** Just as he was about to be brought into the barracks, Paul said to the commander, "May I say something to you?" The commander replied, "You speak Greek?

**38** Then you are not the Egyptian who recently stirred up a revolt and led four thousand assassins out into the wilderness?"

**39** Paul answered, "I am a Jew, from Tarsus in Cilicia — a citizen of no insignificant city. I ask you, let me speak to the people."

**40** When the commander gave him permission, Paul stood on the steps and motioned with his hand to the crowd. A deep silence fell, and he began to address them in the Hebrew language.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "torn ourselves away" (apoспаsthentаs):** The Greek carries the force of being ripped apart, a wrenching separation. This is not a casual departure; it echoes the emotional intensity of the Miletus farewell in chapter 20. The word suggests the parting cost them something visceral.

**Verse 4 — "Through the Spirit they kept urging":** The Sinaiticus text reads dia tou pneumatos, indicating the prophetic warnings came through the Spirit's prompting. These believers did not merely offer human advice; they spoke from divine revelation. Yet Paul still went, distinguishing between the Spirit's warning of what would happen and a direct command not to go.

**Verse 11 — Agabus's prophetic theater:** Agabus does not merely predict; he enacts. Taking Paul's belt and binding himself is classic Old Testament prophetic symbolism (cf. Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel). The dramatic gesture makes the prophecy visceral, forcing the witnesses to see the coming violence before it happens.

**Verse 13 — "breaking my heart" (synthrуptontes mou tеn kardian):** Literally "crushing my heart." Paul does not rebuke their love; he names its cost. Their tears are physically painful to him, yet he will not be moved. His readiness extends beyond imprisonment to death itself — a total surrender rendered here with the raw force of the Greek.

**Verse 14 — "The Lord's will be done":** This echoes Gethsemane (Luke 22:42). The community surrenders its own desires to divine sovereignty, just as Jesus did. The phrase marks the moment the argument ends — not because they agree, but because they yield.

**Verse 20 — "how many thousands" (posai muriades):** The Greek muriades means "tens of thousands," not merely "thousands." This is an enormous number meant to impress upon Paul the political reality: a massive Jewish-Christian population deeply loyal to Torah observance. The scale of the problem shapes everything that follows.

**Verse 28 — "this holy place" (tou hagiou topou toutou):** The accusation mirrors Stephen's trial (Acts 6:13-14). The charge of defiling the temple was a capital offense — Gentiles who crossed the barrier into the inner courts could be executed, and those who brought them in shared the guilt. The accusation is false but explosive.

**Verse 30 — "the gates were slammed shut":** The temple authorities shut the gates to prevent the violence from contaminating sacred space and to prevent Paul from claiming sanctuary. The closing of these doors is both practical and symbolic: the temple is shutting Paul out.

**Verse 36 — "Away with him!" (aire auton):** The same cry will later be directed at Jesus in Luke 23:18. Luke draws a deliberate parallel between Paul's suffering and Christ's Passion, a pattern that runs through the final chapters of Acts.

**Verse 40 — "the Hebrew language" (te Hebraidi dialekto):** Almost certainly Aramaic, the everyday spoken language of Palestinian Jews, though some scholars argue Hebrew. Paul's switch to the local tongue is a strategic move — it signals his identity and credentials as a devout Jew, earning an immediate hush from the hostile crowd.

— PNEUMA
